retraction_title:
<scp>RETRACTION</scp>
                    : A‐Kinase‐Interacting Protein 1 Overexpression Correlates With Deteriorative Tumor Features and Worse Survival Profiles and Promotes Cell Proliferation but Represses Apoptosis in Non‐Small‐Cell Lung Cancer
retraction_abstract: <jats:p>                     <jats:bold>RETRACTION</jats:bold>                     : H. Chen, S. Yan, L. Dong, X. Li, “A‐Kinase‐Interacting Protein 1 Overexpression Correlates With Deteriorative Tumor Features and Worse Survival Profiles and Promotes Cell Proliferation but Represses Apoptosis in Non‐Small‐Cell Lung Cancer,”                     <jats:italic>Journal of Clinical and Laboratory Analysis</jats:italic>                     34, no. 2 (2019): e23061,                     <jats:ext-link xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="https://doi.org/10.1002/jcla.23061">https://doi.org/10.1002/jcla.23061</jats:ext-link>                     .                   </jats:p>                   <jats:p>                     The above article, published online on 11 December 2019 in Wiley Online Library (                     <jats:ext-link xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="http://wileyonlinelibrary.com">wileyonlinelibrary.com</jats:ext-link>                     ), has been retracted by agreement between the journal Editor‐in‐Chief, Rong Fu; and Wiley Periodicals, LLC. The retraction has been agreed due to concerns raised by a third party, which identified compromised data and systematic issues in the published article. The authors and their institutes were unresponsive when these concerns were brought to their attention. Accordingly, the article is retracted as the editors have lost trust in the reliability of the data and the results presented.
original_title: RETRACTED: A‐kinase‐interacting protein 1 overexpression correlates with deteriorative tumor features and worse survival profiles, and promotes cell proliferation but represses apoptosis in non‐small‐cell lung cancer
screen_summary: The retraction notice cites compromised data and systematic issues but does not describe a specific methodological error.
screen_rationale: The notice only mentions general concerns about data reliability and systematic issues without detailing any specific methodological mistake such as a statistical error or design flaw. Therefore, it does not meet the criteria for inclusion.

                  1648 032023)
retraction_abstract: <jats:p>This article has been retracted by IOP Publishing following an allegation that raises concerns this article may have been created, manipulated, and/or sold by a commercial entity. In addition, IOP Publishing has seen no evidence that reliable peer review was conducted on this article, despite the clear standards expected of and communicated to conference organisers.</jats:p>                   <jats:p>The authors of the article have been given opportunity to present evidence that they were the original and genuine creators of the work, however at the time of publication of this notice, IOP Publishing has not received any response. IOP Publishing has analysed the article and agrees there are enough indicators to cause serious doubts over the legitimacy of the work and agree this article should be retracted. The authors are encouraged to contact IOP Publishing Limited if they have any comments on this retraction.</jats:p>                   <jats:p>Retraction published:  16 September 2022</jats:p>

                  1916 012122)
retraction_abstract: <jats:p>This article (and all articles in the proceedings volume relating to the same conference) has been retracted by IOP Publishing following an extensive investigation in line with the COPE guidelines. This investigation has uncovered evidence of systematic manipulation of the publication process and considerable citation manipulation.</jats:p>                   <jats:p>IOP Publishing respectfully requests that readers consider all work within this volume potentially unreliable, as the volume has not been through a credible peer review process.</jats:p>                   <jats:p>                     IOP Publishing regrets that our usual quality checks did not identify these issues before publication, and have since put additional measures in place to try to prevent these issues from reoccurring. IOP Publishing wishes to credit anonymous whistleblowers and the                     <jats:ext-link xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" ext-link-type="uri" xlink:href="https://www.irit.fr/~Guillaume.Cabanac/problematic-paper-screener/" xlink:type="simple">Problematic Paper Screener</jats:ext-link>                     [1] for bringing some of the above issues to our attention, prompting us to investigate further.                   </jats:p>                   <jats:p>                     [1] Cabanac G, Labbé C and Magazinov A 2021 arXiv:                     <jats:ext-link xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" ext-link-type="uri" xlink:href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2107.067

retraction_title: Retraction
retraction_abstract: <jats:p>                     <jats:bold>‘</jats:bold>                     A meta‐analysis and theoretical critique of oxytocin and psychosis: Prospects for attachment and compassion in promoting recovery’ by Andrew Gumley, Christine Braehler, and Angus Macbeth (2014).                   </jats:p>                   <jats:p>                     The above article, published online on 21 February 2014 in Wiley Online Library (                     <jats:ext-link xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="http://www.wileyonlinelibrary.com">wileyonlinelibrary.com</jats:ext-link>                     ), and in Volume 53, pp. 42–61, has been retracted by agreement between the authors, the journal Editor‐in‐Chief, Julie Henry, and John Wiley & Sons Limited.                   </jats:p>                   <jats:p>The retraction has been agreed due to errors in the coding of effects and in the random effects components of the meta‐analysis. In particular, confidence intervals for the random effects estimates were reported as significant, whereas they should have crossed zero, rendering them non‐significant.</jats:p>                   <jats:p>                     <jats:bold>References</jats:bold>                   </jats:p>                   <jats:p>                     <jats:mixed-citation publication-type="journal">                       <jats:string-name>                         <jats:surname>Gumley</jats:surname>                         ,                         <jats:give
original_title: Retracted: A meta‐analysis and theoretical critique of oxytocin and psychosis: Prospects for attachment and compassion in promoting recovery
screen_summary: Errors in coding of effects and in random effects components of the meta-analysis led to confidence intervals being reported as significant when they should have crossed zero.
screen_rationale: The retraction notice explicitly describes a methodological error (incorrect coding and random effects analysis) rather than misconduct, and it specifies that confidence intervals were miscalculated, which is a detail that can be checked by re-reading the manuscript's reported statistics. The error is text-detectable because it involves reported confidence intervals and significance claims that are present in the published paper.

retraction_title: Retraction: Determinants of self-reported adherence to inhaler therapy in patients with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease
retraction_abstract: <jats:p>On behalf of the coauthors and with much regret, I must retract our publication entitled "Determinants of self-reported adherence to inhaler therapy in patients with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease", published in Multidisciplinary Respiratory Medicine 2020;15:654 DOI: https://doi.org/10.4081/mrm.2020.654 The reason for the retraction is a complaint by MMAS Research LLC because of the breaching of the IRS Morisky Widget License. Prof. Caterina BuccaRetired Professor of Respiratory MedicineDept. Medical SciencesUniversity of TurinItaly</jats:p>

retraction_title: Combination restoration in full mouth rehabilitation
retraction_abstract: <jats:p>Successful restoration of the dentition requires plenty of contemporary and conventional treatment techniques and planning and attachment retained partial dentures are one such kind of treatment modality in prosthodontics. Satisfactory restoration in a patient with a partially edentulous situation can be challenging especially when unilateral or bilateral posterior segment of teeth is missing. One such treatment modality is attachment-retained cast partial dentures. The purpose of this article is to provide an overview of a case with maxillary complete denture and opposing cast partial denture with precision attachment.</jats:p>
original_title: Retraction: Combination restoration in full mouth rehabilitation

retraction_title: Controlled hypotension in day care functional endoscopic sinus surgery: A comparison between esmolol and dexmedetomidine: A prospective, double-blind, and randomized study
retraction_abstract: <jats:sec>                     <jats:title>Background:</jats:title>                     <jats:p>Functional endoscopic sinus surgery (FESS) is the cornerstone of therapeutic management for nasal pathologies. This study is to compare the ability of preoperative and intraoperative esmolol versus dexmedetomidine for producing induced hypotension during FESS in adults in a day care setting.</jats:p>                   </jats:sec>                   <jats:sec>                     <jats:title>Materials and Methods:</jats:title>                     <jats:p>                       Sixty patients (20-45 years) posted for FESS under general anesthesia were randomly divided into Group E (                       <jats:italic toggle="yes">n</jats:italic>                       = 30) receiving esmolol, loading dose 1 mg/kg over 1 min followed by 0.5 mg/kg/h infusion during maintenance and Group D (                       <jats:italic toggle="yes">n</jats:italic>                       = 30) receiving dexmedetomidine 1 μg/kg over 15 min before induction of anesthesia followed by 0.5 μg/Kg/h infusion during maintenance, respectively. Nasal bleeding and Surgeon's satisfaction score; amount and number of patients receiving fentanyl and nitroglycerine for analgesia and deliberate hypotension, Postanesthesia Care Unit (PACU) and hospital stay; hemodynamic parameters and side effects were recorded for each patient.                     </jats:p>                   </jats:sec>                   <jats:sec>
